definition: Current mainstream server DRAM standard — higher bandwidth and density than DDR4, with on-DIMM power management and better RAS. The baseline for Genoa/Turin and 4th/5th Gen Xeon.
A DIMM that multiplexes two ranks to roughly double effective data rate over standard DDR5, boosting bandwidth for memory-bound HPC/AI. Supported on platforms like Granite Rapids.
Stacked DRAM placed on-package next to a GPU/accelerator, connected by a wide interface for enormous bandwidth. The critical, supply-constrained memory for AI accelerators.
The enhanced HBM3 generation with higher per-stack bandwidth and capacity, used in H200, Blackwell, and MI325X/MI350. Its availability gates AI-accelerator production.
The next HBM generation with a wider (2048-bit) interface and higher bandwidth/capacity, targeted for next-gen accelerators like NVIDIA Rubin and AMD MI400.
Low-power DRAM (LPDDR5X) used in tightly integrated CPUs like NVIDIA Grace; LPCAMM is a modular, serviceable LPDDR form factor bringing low-power memory to more systems.
The growing gap between compute throughput and the memory bandwidth/capacity needed to feed it. Drives HBM, MRDIMM, and CXL — for AI, memory is often the real bottleneck.
